1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

3	\chapter{The line bundle $\mathcal O_C(P)$ and its global sections (RR.3)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6, p.~144 (definition
7	% of $\mathscr L(D)$); II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective divisors
8	% as divisors of zeros of global sections); IV.1, pp.~294--297
9	% (Riemann--Roch, Lemma~1.2, Examples~1.3.3--1.3.5)
10	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 161,
11	% 174, 311--314). The verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks
12	% below were copied character-by-character from the rendered page images
13	% of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
14	
15	\section{Setup and motivation}
16	
17	This chapter is the third of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
18	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
19	\[
20	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
21	  \,\colon\,
22	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
23	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
24	\]
25	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
26	the Weil-divisor group $\Div(C)$, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C)
27	\to \mathbb Z$, and the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point
28	(\texttt{RR.1}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}); the Riemann--Roch
29	dimension formula in genus~$0$
30	\[
31	  \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0
32	\]
33	via the Euler-characteristic identity
34	(\texttt{RR.2}, \Cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}); the global-sections
35	calculation $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a smooth
36	proper genus-$0$ curve (this chapter, \texttt{RR.3}); the
37	``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$'' classification via
38	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
39	
40	The contribution of \texttt{RR.3} is twofold. First, it constructs the
41	invertible sheaf $\mathcal O_C(P)$ associated to a closed point $P \in C$
42	on a smooth proper curve, identifies its $\bar k$-vector space of global
43	sections with the \emph{Riemann--Roch space}
44	\[
45	  L([P]) \;=\;
46	  \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0\bigr\}
47	  \,\cup\, \{0\},
48	\]
49	and computes its dimension as $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$
50	in the genus-$0$ specialisation. Second, it deduces from the
51	two-dimensionality that $C$ admits a non-constant rational function $f$
52	having at most a simple pole at $P$ and no other poles. This rational
53	function is the seed for \texttt{RR.4}: the pair $(1, f) \in
54	H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))^{\oplus 2}$ generates the sheaf and, via
55	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}, produces the morphism
56	$C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ whose degree-$1$ identification is the
57	content of \texttt{RR.4}.
58	
59	% NOTE (Direct cohomological vanishing vs. Serre duality — iter-174
60	% sub-phase choice). The dimension computation
61	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ would, in Hartshorne's
62	% original presentation, route through Serre duality
63	% $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes \mathcal L^{-1})^*$
64	% to vanish $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ from the degree count
65	% $\deg(K_C - [P]) = 2g - 2 - 1 = -3 < 0$. Mathlib (snapshot
66	% \texttt{b80f227}) ships no dualizing sheaf $\omega_X$ on a scheme and
67	% no Serre duality theorem. The genus-$0$ specialisation does \emph{not}
68	% require Serre duality: the vanishing $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$ is
69	% delivered by the long exact sequence of the structural short exact
70	% sequence
71	% \[
72	%   0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
73	% \]
74	% together with the genus-$0$ hypothesis
75	% $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g = 0$ and the skyscraper
76	% identity $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$. The chapter therefore uses the direct
77	% cohomological vanishing route exclusively; Serre duality is left for
78	% the post-RR-bridge follow-up chapter that handles the general-$g$
79	% statement.
80	
81	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, $\bar k$ is an
82	algebraically closed field and $C$ is a smooth proper geometrically
83	irreducible curve over $\bar k$. Concretely, the Lean signatures
84	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OCofP.lean}) carry the same
85	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API:
86	\begin{quote}
87	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
88	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
89	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
90	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
91	\end{quote}
92	The genus $g(C)$ is the one defined in \Cref{def:genus} of
93	\texttt{Genus.tex}; the divisor $[P]$ of a closed point is the one of
94	\Cref{def:divisor_closed_point} of \texttt{RiemannRoch\_WeilDivisor.tex};
95	the principal divisor $\div(f)$ of a nonzero rational function and the
96	order $\ord_Q(f)$ at a prime divisor are the ones of
97	\Cref{def:principal_divisor} and \Cref{def:order_at_point}.

99	\section{The line bundle of a closed point on a curve}
100	
101	\begin{definition}
102	
103	[Line bundle of a closed point]
104	  \label{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
105	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint}
106	  \uses{def:divisor_closed_point, def:codim1_cycles, def:prime_divisor,
107	        def:order_at_point}
108	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~144 (definition of the invertible
109	  % sheaf $\mathscr L(D)$ associated to a Cartier divisor) (read from
110	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 161).
111	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $D$ be a Cartier divisor on a scheme
112	  % $X$, represented by $\{(U_i, f_i)\}$ as above. We define a subsheaf
113	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ of the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathscr K$ by
114	  % taking $\mathscr L(D)$ to be the sub-$\mathscr O_X$-module of
115	  % $\mathscr K$ generated by $f_i^{-1}$ on $U_i$. This is well-defined,
116	  % since $f_i/f_j$ is invertible on $U_i \cap U_j$, and $f_i^{-1}$ and
117	  % $f_j^{-1}$ generate the same $\mathscr O_X$-module. We call
118	  % $\mathscr L(D)$ the \emph{sheaf associated to $D$}."
119	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.13. \emph{Let $X$ be a scheme. Then:
120	  % (a) for any Cartier divisor $D$, $\mathscr L(D)$ is an invertible
121	  % sheaf on $X$.}"
122	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~144 (definition of
123	  $\mathscr L(D)$ and Proposition~6.13(a)).}
124	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
125	  closed point. Let $\mathfrak m_P \subset \mathcal O_{C, P}$ denote the
126	  maximal ideal of the local ring at $P$; by smoothness of $C$,
127	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation ring and $\mathfrak m_P$
128	  is generated by any uniformizer $f_P \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$ (an
129	  element of $\mathfrak m_P \setminus \mathfrak m_P^2$). The Cartier
130	  divisor associated to the closed point $P$ is the effective
131	  Cartier divisor cut out locally by the uniformizer $f_P$ on an open
132	  affine neighbourhood of $P$, and by the unit $1$ on the complement
133	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. The \emph{line bundle of the closed point $P$},
134	  denoted $\mathcal O_C(P)$, is the locally free $\mathcal O_C$-module
135	  of rank $1$ defined by Hartshorne's construction $\mathscr L(D)$
136	  applied to this Cartier divisor: explicitly, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
137	  sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of the sheaf of total quotient rings
138	  $\mathcal K_C$ (the constant sheaf of the function field $K(C)$ on the
139	  integral scheme $C$) that is generated by $f_P^{-1} \in K(C)$ on a
140	  neighbourhood of $P$ and by $1 \in K(C)$ on the complement
141	  $C \setminus \{P\}$. Hartshorne's Proposition~6.13(a) confirms that
142	  this subsheaf is an invertible sheaf, independently of the choice of
143	  uniformizer $f_P$ (any two uniformizers differ by a unit in
144	  $\mathcal O_{C, P}^{\times}$, so they generate the same
145	  $\mathcal O_C$-submodule of $\mathcal K_C$).
146	
147	  \paragraph{Equivalent description via the ideal sheaf.} Let
148	  $\mathcal I_P \subset \mathcal O_C$ denote the ideal sheaf of the
149	  closed point $P$ (locally generated by the uniformizer $f_P$ near
150	  $P$, and equal to $\mathcal O_C$ on the complement). Then
151	  $\mathcal I_P$ is the line bundle $\mathcal O_C(-P)$ associated to the
152	  Weil divisor $-[P]$, and $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the
153	  $\mathcal O_C$-linear dual:
154	  \[
155	    \mathcal O_C(P) \;\cong\; \mathcal H\!om_{\mathcal O_C}(\mathcal I_P,
156	    \mathcal O_C)
157	    \;\cong\; \mathcal I_P^{-1}.
158	  \]
159	  Both descriptions yield the same invertible sheaf; the
160	  ``subsheaf of $\mathcal K_C$'' description above is the one used in
161	  the prose, while the ``dual of the ideal sheaf'' description is the
162	  most direct entry point for the Lean construction (Mathlib's
163	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData} packages
164	  quasi-coherent ideal sheaves, from which the dual is constructed via
165	  the internal-hom on invertible sheaves).
166	
167	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
168	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint} takes a
169	  scheme $C$, a point $P : C$, and a closedness witness
170	  $\texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}$ (the same input shape as
171	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), and returns an invertible
172	  $\mathcal O_C$-module $\mathcal O_C(P)$. The well-definedness as an
173	  invertible sheaf is part of the construction and goes through
174	  smoothness of $C$ (delivering the DVR stalk at $P$) and the standard
175	  ideal-sheaf-of-a-closed-point machinery in Mathlib's
176	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf} chapter. The chapter's
177	  consumption of the closed-point hypothesis matches \texttt{RR.1}:
178	  closed plus dimension-$1$-integral delivers $\codim_C \{P\} = 1$
179	  automatically. The declaration is \texttt{noncomputable}.
180	\end{definition}

182	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
183	
184	\begin{lemma}
185	
186	[Global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as rational functions with a
187	controlled pole]
188	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
190	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
191	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
192	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
193	  % divisors linearly equivalent to $D_0$ are the divisors of zeros of
194	  % global sections of $\mathscr L(D_0)$, with the explicit
195	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
196	  % $f$ satisfying $(f) \geq -D_0$) (read from
197	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
198	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let $X$ be a nonsingular
199	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field $k$. Let
200	  % $D_0$ be a divisor on $X$ and let $\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)$
201	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
202	  % (a) for each nonzero $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$, the divisor of
203	  % zeros $(s)_0$ is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$;
204	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to $D_0$ is $(s)_0$
205	  % for some $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$; and
206	  % (c) two sections $s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)$ have the same
207	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a $\lambda \in k^*$ such
208	  % that $s' = \lambda s$.}"
209	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If $D \geq 0$ and $D = D_0 + (f)$, then
210	  % $(f) \geq -D_0$. Thus $f$ gives a global section of
211	  % $\mathscr L(D_0)$ whose divisor of zeros is $D$."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
213	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
214	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$, let $P \in C$ be a
215	  closed point, and let $\mathcal O_C(P)$ be the line bundle of
216	  \Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}. The $\bar k$-vector space of
217	  global sections of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is naturally identified with the
218	  Riemann--Roch space
219	  \[
220	    L([P]) \;:=\;
221	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
222	    \text{ in } \Div(C)\bigr\}
223	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
224	  \]
225	  Equivalently, $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ defines a global section of
226	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if and only if
227	  \[
228	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
229	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
230	    \qquad
231	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1.
232	  \]
233	  Under this identification, the inclusion
234	  $\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of constant functions
235	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
236	  \mathcal O_C(P)$ exhibiting $\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
237	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor $[P]$.
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	\begin{proof}
241	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:order_at_point,
242	        def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_closed_point}
243	  By construction, $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module of
244	  the sheaf of total quotient rings $\mathcal K_C$ generated locally by
245	  $f_P^{-1}$ near $P$ and by $1$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$, where $f_P$ is
246	  a uniformizer at $P$ (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}). Since $C$
247	  is integral, $\mathcal K_C$ is the constant sheaf at the function field
248	  $K(C)$, and a global section of any sub-$\mathcal O_C$-module
249	  $\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C$ is precisely an element of $K(C)$
250	  whose germ at every point lies in $\mathcal F$ at that point.
251	  Therefore $f \in K(C)$ is a global section of $\mathcal O_C(P)$ if
252	  and only if:
253	
254	  \begin{itemize}
255	    \item For every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$, the germ of
256	      $f$ at $Q$ lies in $\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}$
257	      (the structure sheaf, since $\mathcal O_C(P)$ agrees with
258	      $\mathcal O_C$ on $C \setminus \{P\}$); equivalently,
259	      $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$.
260	    \item At the closed point $P$, the germ of $f$ at $P$ lies in
261	      $\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}$;
262	      equivalently, $f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}$, i.e.\
263	      $\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0$, i.e.\ $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$.
264	  \end{itemize}
265	  In divisorial language (\Cref{def:principal_divisor},
266	  \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}), the two conditions combine to
267	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
268	  $L([P])$. The zero element of $K(C)$ is included by convention as the
269	  zero global section.
270	
271	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
272	  taking $D_0 = [P]$ effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
273	  rational function $f$ with $(f) \geq -D_0$ defines a global section
274	  of $\mathscr L(D_0)$. The condition $(f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]$ is exactly
275	  $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$. The compatibility with the constant inclusion
276	  follows by taking $f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}$: then $\div(1) = 0$, so
277	  $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, and the corresponding section of
278	  $\mathcal O_C(P)$ is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
279	  \cong \bar k$ under the structural inclusion $\mathcal O_C
280	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
281	\end{proof}

283	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
284	
285	\begin{lemma}
286	
287	[Vanishing of $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
288	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
289	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
290	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
291	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
292	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
293	  % step ``Next, let $D$ be any divisor, and let $P$ be any point.''):
294	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
295	  % k(P) \to 0$ and its tensor with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
296	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$
297	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
298	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider $P$ as a closed subscheme of $X$. Its
299	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf $k$ sitting at the point $P$,
300	  % which we denote by $k(P)$, and its ideal sheaf is $\mathscr L(-P)$
301	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
302	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0$.
303	  % Tensoring with $\mathscr L(D + P)$ we get
304	  % $0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0$,
305	  % (since $\mathscr L(P)$ is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
306	  % does not affect the sheaf $k(P)$)."
307	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
308	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
309	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
310	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
311	  \[
312	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
313	  \]
314	  i.e.\ $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$.
315	\end{lemma}
316	
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
319	        def:genus, def:Scheme_HModule}
320	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at $D = 0$. The standard short
321	  exact sequence
322	  \[
323	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
324	  \]
325	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
326	  subscheme $P$ is $\mathcal O_C(-[P])$; the quotient is the structure
327	  sheaf of $P$, which is the skyscraper sheaf $k(P) \cong \bar k$
328	  supported at $P$ since $P$ has residue field $\bar k$ by closedness
329	  over the algebraically closed base $\bar k$). Tensoring with the
330	  locally free rank-$1$ sheaf $\mathcal O_C([P])$
331	  (\Cref{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint}) preserves exactness and leaves
332	  the skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ unchanged, giving
333	  \[
334	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
335	  \]
336	  in $\mathbf{Coh}(C)$. Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
337	  cohomology (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}, the project's $\Module \bar
338	  k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that $\dim C = 1$ kills
339	  $H^i$ for $i \geq 2$ (Grothendieck vanishing on a $1$-dimensional
340	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
341	  \begin{multline*}
342	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
343	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
344	  \end{multline*}
345	  Two ingredients close the proof:
346	
347	  \emph{(a) $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$.} This is the genus-$0$
348	  hypothesis: by \Cref{def:genus},
349	  \[
350	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
351	  \]
352	  so $g(C) = 0$ is exactly $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$,
353	  i.e.\ $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
354	
355	  \emph{(b) $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.} The skyscraper sheaf $k(P)$ is
356	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
357	  on an open $U \subset C$ either restricts to $0$ (if $P \notin U$) or
358	  takes values in the stalk $k(P)_P \cong \bar k$ (if $P \in U$), and
359	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
360	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
361	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
362	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$, and \emph{a fortiori}
363	  $H^1(C, k(P)) = 0$.
364	
365	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
366	  segment ending at $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ to
367	  \[
368	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
369	  \]
370	  whence $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$, as required.
371	
372	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
373	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
374	  $\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)$ at the abelian-group level
375	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
376	  project's $\Module \bar k$-flavoured cohomology pipeline
377	  (\Cref{def:Scheme_HModule}) inherits the long exact sequence by
378	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
379	  $H^i(C, k(P)) = 0$ for $i \geq 1$ is closed by either the flasque
380	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
381	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
382	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
383	  $C \setminus \{P\}$ and a small affine open around $P$ (the
384	  skyscraper has zero sections on $C \setminus \{P\}$, so the Čech
385	  complex degenerates).
386	\end{proof}

390	\begin{theorem}
391	
392	[$\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ on a genus-$0$ curve]
393	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
395	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
396	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
397	        def:eulerChar_curve,

399	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
400	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
401	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with $D = [P]$: $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
402	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
403	  % $H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0$ (read from
404	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
405	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let $D$ be a
406	  % divisor on a curve $X$ of genus $g$. Then}
407	  % $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$."
408	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any $D$,
409	  % $\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g$,
410	  % where for any coherent sheaf $\mathscr F$ on $X$, $\chi(\mathscr F)$
411	  % is the Euler characteristic
412	  % $\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)$."
413	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
414	  $\chi$-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the

416	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
417	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
418	  \[

422	
423	\begin{proof}
424	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint, def:divisor_closed_point,
425	        def:divisor_degree, def:genus,
426	        def:eulerChar_curve,
427	        thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus,
428	        lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
429	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
430	  \Cref{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus} of \texttt{RR.2} to
431	  the divisor $D = [P]$ of \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}. The
432	  degree of $[P]$ is $\deg([P]) = 1$ (every closed point contributes
433	  $1$ to the bare-sum degree of \Cref{def:divisor_degree}), and the
434	  genus hypothesis $g(C) = 0$ (\Cref{def:genus}) gives
435	  \[
436	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
437	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
438	  \]
439	  Unfolding $\chi$ via \Cref{def:eulerChar_curve} as a two-term Euler
440	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
441	  \[
442	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
443	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
444	    \;=\; 2.
445	  \]
446	  By
447	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}, the second
448	  term vanishes:
449	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0$. Substituting yields
450	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, as required.
451	
452	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
453	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
454	  \Cref{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero} computes
455	  the dimension of $H^0$ in one stroke without invoking
456	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
457	  \[
458	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
459	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
460	  \]
461	  together with $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1$
462	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: $H^0$ of the structure sheaf on a proper
463	  geometrically irreducible variety over $\bar k$ is $\bar k$),
464	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1$ (skyscraper at a $\bar k$-rational
465	  point), and $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$ (genus-$0$), the connecting
466	  map $H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ has zero codomain and the
467	  segment yields the short exact sequence
468	  \[
469	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
470	  \]
471	  of $\bar k$-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
472	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
473	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
474	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
475	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
476	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
477	\end{proof}

479	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
480	
481	\begin{corollary}
482	
483	[A genus-$0$ curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
484	single simple pole]
485	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
486	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
487	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
488	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
490	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
491	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
492	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
493	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
494	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
495	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
496	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
497	  % point on a genus-$0$ curve, as a direct consequence of the
498	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
499	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
500	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let $X$ be a curve, and let $P \in X$ be a
501	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
502	  % K(X)$, which is regular everywhere except at $P$."
503	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
504	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
505	  $\bar k$ with $g(C) = 0$, and let $P \in C$ be a closed point. Then
506	  there exists a non-constant rational function $f \in K(C)$ such that:
507	  \begin{itemize}
508	    \item $f$ is regular on $C \setminus \{P\}$, i.e.\ $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
509	      for every closed point $Q \in C$ with $Q \neq P$;
510	    \item $f$ has at most a simple pole at $P$, i.e.\
511	      $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$;
512	    \item $f \notin \bar k$, equivalently
513	      $\div(f) \neq 0 \in \Div(C)$.
514	  \end{itemize}
515	  In particular, the pair $(1, f)$ spans
516	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ as a $\bar k$-vector space.
517	\end{corollary}
518	
519	\begin{proof}
520	  \uses{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint,
521	        def:divisor_closed_point, def:order_at_point,
522	        def:principal_divisor, def:genus,
523	        thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero,
524	        lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
525	  By
526	  \Cref{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero},
527	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$. The constant subspace
528	  $\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$, obtained from the
529	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
530	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
531	  $f = 1$, with $\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0$, under the
532	  identification of
533	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}), is a one-dimensional
534	  $\bar k$-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension $2$,
535	  the quotient $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1$ is a
536	  non-zero one-dimensional $\bar k$-vector space; any lift to
537	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ of a non-zero element of the quotient is
538	  an element of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1$.
539	
540	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
541	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
542	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff} the section
543	  $s$ corresponds to a rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$
544	  satisfying $\div(f) + [P] \geq 0$, equivalently $\ord_Q(f) \geq 0$
545	  for every $Q \neq P$ and $\ord_P(f) \geq -1$. The choice
546	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ means $f$ is not the image of a constant,
547	  so $f \notin \bar k$ (every non-zero element of
548	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k$ corresponds, under the
549	  identification, to a constant element of $K(C)$). The three bullets
550	  of the statement are now immediate.
551	
552	  Spanning. Linear independence of $1$ and $f$ over $\bar k$ in
553	  $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ is the same condition as
554	  $s \notin \bar k \cdot 1$ above; combined with the dimension count
555	  $\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$, the pair $(1, f)$ is a
556	  $\bar k$-basis of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$.
557	
558	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
559	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
560	  returns an element $f$ of the project's function-field carrier
561	  $K(C)$ together with proofs of regularity on $C \setminus \{P\}$ and
562	  the simple-pole bound at $P$, packaged in the same shape as
563	  \Cref{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}'s output. The
564	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
565	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
566	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
567	\end{proof}

62	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
63	
64	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
65	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
66	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
67	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
68	`K(X)`.
69	
70	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
71	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
72	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
73	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
74	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
75	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
76	-/
77	
78	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
79	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
80	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
81	
82	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
83	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
84	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
85	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
86	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
87	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
88	integrality field is needed.
89	
90	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
91	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
92	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
93	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
94	  point : X
95	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
96	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
97	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
98	
99	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
100	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
101	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
102	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
103	
104	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
105	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
106	
107	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
108	
109	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
110	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
111	
112	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
113	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
114	
115	end Scheme.WeilDivisor

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-173+: the body extracts the DVR `O_{X,η}` (from `(*)` regularity), reads off the
138	`addVal` (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal : R → ℕ∞`), extends it to the fraction field
139	`K(X)`, and produces an `ℤ`-valued valuation on `K(X)^×`. -/
140	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
141	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
142	  sorry
143	
144	end Scheme.RationalMap

146	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
147	
148	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}

150	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
151	
152	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
153	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
154	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
155	
156	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
157	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
158	
159	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
160	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
161	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
162	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
163	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
164	
165	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
166	
167	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
168	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
169	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
170	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
171	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
172	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
173	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
174	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
175	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
176	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
177	hypothesised regime. -/
178	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
179	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
180	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
181	
182	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
183	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
184	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
185	multiplicity one. -/
186	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
187	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
188	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
189	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
190	
191	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
192	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
193	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
194	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
195	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
196	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
197	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
198	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
199	
200	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
201	
202	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
203	
204	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
205	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
206	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
207	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
208	
209	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
210	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
211	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
212	
213	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
214	
215	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
216	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
217	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
218	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
219	
220	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
221	
222	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
223	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
224	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
225	
226	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
227	
228	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
229	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
230	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
231	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
232	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
233	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
234	
235	@[simp]
236	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
237	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
238	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
239	
240	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
241	
242	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
243	
244	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
245	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
246	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
247	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
248	
249	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
250	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
253	following definition, p. 131).
254	
255	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
256	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
257	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
258	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
259	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
260	  sorry
261	
262	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
263	
264	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
265	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
266	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
267	
268	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
269	
270	iter-173+: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`,
271	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
272	union of supports. -/
273	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
274	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
275	  sorry
276	
277	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
278	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
279	
280	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
281	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
282	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
283	
284	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
285	
286	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
287	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
288	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
289	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
290	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
291	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
292	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
293	iters of `RR.1`. -/
294	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
295	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
296	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
297	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
298	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
299	  sorry
300	
301	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
302	
303	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
304	
305	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
306	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
307	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
308	
309	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
310	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
311	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
312	**divisor class group** of `X`.
313	
314	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
315	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
316	
317	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
318	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
319	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
320	
321	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
322	
323	end AlgebraicGeometry

100	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
101	
102	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
103	curve `C / k̄`.**
104	
105	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
106	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
107	alternating sum
108	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
109	expression
110	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
111	
112	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
113	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
114	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
115	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
116	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
117	
118	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
119	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
120	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
121	
122	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
123	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
124	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
125	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
126	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
127	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
128	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
129	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
130	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
131	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
132	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)

173	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
174	
175	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
176	`C / k̄`.**
177	
178	By definition,
179	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
180	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
181	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
182	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
183	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
184	of `genus C`.
185	
186	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
187	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
188	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
191	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
192	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
193	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
194	
195	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
196	
197	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
198	
199	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
200	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
201	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
202	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
203	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
204	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
205	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
206	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
207	body. -/
208	
209	/-- **Euler-characteristic identity for `𝒪_C(D)` on a smooth proper curve
210	of genus `g`.**
211	
212	For every Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)`,
213	`χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g(C)`.
214	
215	The proof is Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3's reduction: induction on the
216	free-abelian-group structure of `Div(C)` on closed points, base case `D =
217	0` giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` (the `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` is the Hartshorne
218	I.3.4 input via the project's `H⁰`-bridge), and inductive step via
219	additivity of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence
220	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`.
221	
222	Blueprint reference: `thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus`
223	(Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3, p. 295). -/
224	theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
225	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
226	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
227	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
228	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
229	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
230	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D)
231	      = (Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D) + 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) :=
232	  sorry
233	
234	/-! ## §5. The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero -/
235	
236	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
237	
238	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
239	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
240	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
241	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
242	
243	The proof specialises the χ-identity
244	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
245	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
246	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
247	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
248	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
249	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
250	
251	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
252	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
253	theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
254	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
255	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
256	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
257	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
258	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
259	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
260	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
261	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :
262	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.l (C := C) D : ℤ)
263	      = Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D + 1 :=
264	  sorry
265	
266	end AlgebraicGeometry

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK
8	
9	/-!
10	# Genus of a smooth proper curve
11	
12	The genus of a smooth, proper, geometrically irreducible curve over a field.
13	
14	## Status (closed iteration 011 — `genus`)
15	
16	The iter-011 refactor added the `noncomputable` modifier (user-authorised
17	`2026-05-07`) and an import of `AlgebraicJacobian.Cohomology.StructureSheafModuleK`
18	so the body uses the project's `ModuleCat k`-flavoured cohomology
19	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009) of the structure sheaf
20	`Scheme.toModuleKSheaf` (iter-006). The body is the probe-confirmed
21	one-liner
22	
23	    Module.finrank k (Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1)
24	
25	which is the honest mathematical definition `dim_k H^1(C, O_C)`.
26	
27	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Genus.tex` for the informal proof sketch and
28	`.archon/STRATEGY.md` for the multi-phase build-out plan.
29	-/
30	
31	set_option autoImplicit false
32	
33	universe u
34	
35	open CategoryTheory Limits
36	
37	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
38	
39	/-- The genus of a smooth proper curve. -/
40	noncomputable def genus {k : Type u} [Field k] (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
41	    [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
42	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : ℕ :=
43	  Module.finrank k (Scheme.HModule k (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 1)
44	
45	end AlgebraicGeometry

99	
100	/-! ## §1. The Euler characteristic of a coherent sheaf on a curve -/
101	
102	/-- **Euler characteristic of a `ModuleCat k`-valued sheaf on a smooth proper
103	curve `C / k̄`.**
104	
105	On a curve (one-dimensional scheme), Grothendieck vanishing
106	(Hartshorne III.2.7) gives `H^i(C, 𝓕) = 0` for `i ≥ 2`, so the classical
107	alternating sum
108	`χ(𝓕) = Σ_{i ≥ 0} (-1)^i dim_{k̄} H^i(C, 𝓕)` collapses to the two-term
109	expression
110	`χ(𝓕) = dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝓕) − dim_{k̄} H¹(C, 𝓕)`.
111	
112	This is the definition we ship. Coherence of `𝓕` on the proper `k̄`-scheme
113	`C` guarantees that both `H⁰` and `H¹` are finite-dimensional `k̄`-vector
114	spaces (Serre's coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem, the same
115	finiteness backing `AlgebraicGeometry.genus`), so the two `Module.finrank`s
116	are honest natural numbers and the difference is a well-defined integer.
117	
118	The `Module k̄`-valued cohomology pipeline is the project's
119	`Scheme.HModule` (iter-009), the same wrapper used by
120	`AlgebraicGeometry.genus`.
121	
122	Blueprint reference: `def:eulerChar_curve`
123	(Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295, displayed inside the proof of Theorem 1.3). -/
124	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
125	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
126	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
127	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
128	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
129	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
130	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
131	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
132	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)
133	
134	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (placeholder)
135	
136	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
137	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of
138	closed points are glued into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. To keep the
139	iter-174 skeleton's pin signatures substantive, we expose `𝒪_C(D)` as a
140	typed-`sorry` `ModuleCat k̄`-valued sheaf-on-`C`. The iter-175+ closure of
141	`RR.3` will replace the body with the construction; downstream pins (the
142	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) consume
143	this placeholder only through its `H⁰` and `H¹`, so their signatures
144	encode arithmetic identities on finiteness-of-cohomology outputs and are
145	not vacuous. -/
146	
147	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
148	
149	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
150	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
151	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
152	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
153	
154	/-- **The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper
155	curve `C / k̄`** (placeholder for `RR.3`).
156	
157	For a Weil divisor `D = Σᵢ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` on a smooth proper geometrically
158	irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the associated invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` is the
159	locally-free `𝒪_C`-module of rank one cut out (locally near each `Pᵢ`) by
160	the `(−nᵢ)`-th power of the local uniformiser at `Pᵢ`. Hartshorne II §6
161	Proposition 6.13 packages this construction; the project-side `RR.3`
162	chapter (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, to be added) will produce the carrier as
163	a `Sheaf` of `ModuleCat k̄`-modules on `C.left`.
164	
165	In the iter-174 skeleton this is a typed-`sorry`. The body in `RR.3` will
166	be the invertible-sheaf glue of the locally principal ideals of the
167	support of `D`. -/
168	noncomputable def sheafOf (_D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
169	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
170	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
171	  sorry
172	
173	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
174	
175	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
176	`C / k̄`.**
177	
178	By definition,
179	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
180	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
181	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
182	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
183	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
184	of `genus C`.
185	
186	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
187	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
188	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
189	
190	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
191	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
192	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
193	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)

88	/-! ## §1. Line bundles on a product
89

91	`O_(C ×_S T)`-module, i.e. a sheaf of modules locally free of rank one. In
92	Mathlib `b80f227` the `Scheme.Modules` category is in place
93	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf`), but there is no `IsInvertible`
94	predicate on its objects yet (the closest is `Module.Invertible R M` for rings,
95	in `Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup`). The iter-174 file-skeleton therefore
96	encodes the type of line bundles on the product by a typed `sorry` at the type
97	level; iter-175+ will instantiate it as a subtype of `(pullback πC πT).Modules`
98	cutting out invertibility.
99
